clti.label: Figure 1
clti.caption: Prevalence of Peripheral Artery Disease by Age and Sex in Different Income Countries
clti.description: This chart illustrates the prevalence of peripheral artery disease (PAD) in various age groups, segmented by sex and income level of countries. It compares high-income countries (HICs) and low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), showing a trend of increasing prevalence with age.

clti.label: Figure 2
clti.caption: Odds Ratios for Peripheral Artery Disease Risk Factors
clti.description: This chart displays the odds ratios (ORs) for various risk factors associated with peripheral artery disease (PAD) in high-income countries (HICs), low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), and globally. Factors include history of cardiovascular disease, CRP levels, cholesterol levels, smoking status, diabetes, hypertension, BMI, sex, and age.

clti.label: Figure 3
clti.caption: Risk Factors and Atherosclerotic Lesion Levels in Lower Limbs
clti.description: This diagram illustrates the association between various risk factors and the levels of atherosclerotic lesions in the lower limbs. The red overlay indicates the correlation of each risk factor with specific patterns of atherosclerotic disease across different leg segments: iliac, femoro-popliteal, and crural.

clti.label: Figure 4
clti.caption: Flowchart for Investigating Suspected CLTI
clti.description: This flowchart outlines the diagnostic process for patients with suspected chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI). It begins with clinical suspicion and guides through physical exams, ABI measurements, and further testing based on results, leading to potential vascular imaging.

clti.label: Figure 5
clti.caption: Imaging Algorithm for CLTI Patients
clti.description: This flowchart outlines the suggested imaging algorithm for patients with chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI) who are candidates for revascularization. It includes steps for using duplex ultrasound, CTA, MRA, and diagnostic catheter angiography to achieve adequate imaging of arterial anatomy.

clti.label: Figure 6
clti.caption: Infra-malleolar/Pedal Descriptor in GLASS
clti.description: This figure presents the infra-malleolar/pedal descriptors used in the Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS) to classify the condition of arteries crossing the ankle into the foot. It includes angiograms illustrating three patterns: P0, P1, and P2.

clti.label: Figure 7
clti.caption: Illustration of Lower Limb Arterial Anatomy and Occlusions
clti.description: This diagram illustrates various anatomical views of the lower limb arteries, highlighting the common femoral artery (CFA), deep femoral artery (DFA), superficial femoral artery (SFA), and popliteal artery (Pop). It shows different patterns of arterial occlusions and stenosis.

clti.label: Figure 8
clti.caption: Infrapopliteal Disease Grading in GLASS System
clti.description: This table illustrates the grading of infrapopliteal disease using the Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS). It categorizes disease severity from Grade 0 to Grade 4, based on the extent of stenosis and chronic total occlusion (CTO) in the tibial arteries.

clti.label: Figure 9
clti.caption: GLASS Stage Assignment Table
clti.description: This table outlines the assignment of Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS) stages based on femoropopliteal (FP) and infrapopliteal (IP) grades. It provides a matrix to determine GLASS stages I to III using angiographic images.

clti.label: Figure 10
clti.caption: Representative Angiograms of GLASS Stage I Disease Patterns
clti.description: This figure shows angiograms illustrating the Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS) stage I disease patterns. The target arterial path (TAP) is highlighted in yellow. Two examples (A and B) demonstrate different grades of femoropopliteal (FP) and infrapopliteal (IP) disease.

clti.label: Figure 11
clti.caption: Angiograms of GLASS Stage II Disease Patterns
clti.description: This figure presents representative angiograms illustrating the Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS) stage II disease patterns. The target arterial path (TAP) is highlighted in yellow, showing the anterior tibial artery and femoropopliteal grade 1 occlusion.

clti.label: Figure 12
clti.caption: Representative Angiograms of GLASS Stage III Patterns
clti.description: This figure displays angiograms illustrating Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS) stage III disease patterns. The target arterial path (TAP) is highlighted in yellow, showing different arterial involvements in two cases. Panel A focuses on the peroneal artery, while Panel B highlights the anterior tibial artery.

clti.label: Figure 13
clti.caption: Flowchart for Revascularization in CLTI Patients Using GLASS
clti.description: This flowchart outlines the steps for applying the Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS) to stage infrainguinal disease in patients with chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI) who are candidates for revascularization. It includes obtaining angiographic imaging, defining the target artery path, grading arterial segments, and determining the revascularization strategy.

clti.label: Figure 14
clti.caption: 3D Paradigm for Evidence-Based Revascularization in CLTI
clti.description: This 3D chart illustrates a paradigm for evidence-based revascularization in the treatment of chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI). It integrates patient risk, limb severity, and anatomic complexity of disease to guide clinical decisions.

clti.label: Figure 15
clti.caption: Clinical Decision-Making for Chronic Limb-Threatening Ischemia
clti.description: This flowchart outlines the decision-making process for managing patients with chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI). It includes assessing limb threat severity, evaluating candidacy for limb salvage, and considering revascularization needs. The process involves staging the disease anatomically and determining the appropriate intervention based on risk assessment.

clti.label: Figure 16
clti.caption: Benefit of Revascularization in CLTI
clti.description: This chart illustrates the benefit of performing revascularization in patients with chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI) based on the severity of ischemia and limb severity. The benefit is categorized into high and low/nil, with color coding for easy interpretation.

clti.label: Figure 17
clti.caption: Revascularization Strategy Matrix for Infrapopliteal Disease
clti.description: This chart presents a matrix for determining the preferred initial revascularization strategy based on limb severity (WIfI stage) and anatomic complexity (GLASS stage). It categorizes treatment options into open bypass, endovascular, indeterminate, and no revascularization.

mesenteric_renal.label: Figure 34
mesenteric_renal.caption: Yun Classification of Visceral Artery Dissection
mesenteric_renal.description: This figure illustrates the Yun classification system for visceral artery dissection as seen on computed tomography angiography. It categorizes dissections into four types based on the visibility of entry and re-entry tears and the status of the false lumen.

vascular_access.label: Figure 1
vascular_access.caption: Renal Replacement Treatment Options Flowchart
vascular_access.description: This flowchart outlines the treatment options for patients with End Stage Renal Disease (ESRD). It includes pathways for transplantation, peritoneal dialysis, and hemodialysis through vascular access, detailing the types of central venous catheters and arteriovenous access options.

vascular_access.label: Figure 2
vascular_access.caption: Kaplan-Meier Curves for AVF Failure by CVC Use and Maturation Time
vascular_access.description: This figure presents Kaplan-Meier survival curves showing the time to arteriovenous fistula (AVF) failure. The left chart compares patients with and without central venous catheters (CVC) at the initiation of hemodialysis, while the right chart compares different time intervals to AVF maturation. The survival probability is plotted over months from first cannulation.

vascular_access.label: Figure 4
vascular_access.caption: Survival Rates for Dialysis Methods Over Time
vascular_access.description: This chart compares the survival rates of patients using peritoneal dialysis (PD), central venous catheters (HD-CVC), and arteriovenous fistulas/grafts (HD-AVF/AVG) over a five-year period. The data is adjusted using a stratified Cox proportional hazards model.

vascular_access.label: Figure 5
vascular_access.caption: Imaging Algorithm for Vascular Access Pathology
vascular_access.description: This flowchart outlines the imaging process for assessing pathological findings in vascular access. It differentiates between peripheral, central venous, and recurrent central venous pathologies, guiding through steps involving DUS, CTA, and DSA for evaluation and treatment.

vascular_access.label: Figure 6
vascular_access.caption: VA Cannulation Techniques Diagram
vascular_access.description: This diagram illustrates three different vascular access cannulation techniques: rope ladder, area, and buttonhole. Each technique is depicted on an arm model to show the needle insertion points and patterns.

vascular_access.label: Figure 7
vascular_access.caption: Techniques for Flow Reduction in Arteriovenous Fistulas
vascular_access.description: This figure illustrates various surgical techniques for reducing blood flow in arteriovenous fistulas to prevent ischemia and manage high flow in upper arm access. It includes methods like banding the vein near the anastomosis and using smaller diameter arteries for inflow.

vascular_access.label: Figure 8
vascular_access.caption: Vascular Access Prosthetic Graft Configurations
vascular_access.description: This figure illustrates various configurations of vascular access prosthetic grafts. It shows different pathways for graft placement from the brachial artery to various veins, including the subclavian and internal jugular veins, using different incision sites.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Table 10
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Classes of Recommendations by ESC
chronic_venous_disease.description: This table outlines the classes of recommendations according to the European Society of Cardiology (ESC). It categorizes treatments and procedures based on their evidence and effectiveness, ranging from beneficial to potentially harmful.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Table 11
chronic_venous_disease.caption: 2020 CEAP Classification Update for Venous Disease
chronic_venous_disease.description: This table presents the updated 2020 CEAP classification for chronic venous disease, detailing clinical, etiological, anatomical, and pathophysiological classes. It provides descriptions for each class, aiding in the standardized reporting and diagnosis of venous conditions.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Table 23
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Effects of Venoactive Drugs on Venous Symptoms and Oedema
chronic_venous_disease.description: This table summarizes the effects of various venoactive drugs on symptoms associated with venous diseases, such as pain, heaviness, and oedema. Each drug is evaluated for its effectiveness in alleviating specific symptoms.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Table 46
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Summary of Techniques for Saphenous Trunk Incompetence Treatment
chronic_venous_disease.description: This table provides a comparative summary of various techniques used to treat saphenous trunk incompetence. It includes information on follow-up duration, effectiveness in reflux abolition, quality of life improvement, need for tumescent anesthesia, and risk of nerve injury.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Table 57
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Causes of Varicose Vein Recurrence After Treatment
chronic_venous_disease.description: This table outlines the main causes of varicose vein recurrence following treatment, categorizing them into tactical errors, technical errors, neovascularization, recanalization, and disease progression. Each category is further explained with specific descriptions of the errors or conditions leading to recurrence.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Figure 3
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Diagnostic and Treatment Pathways for Chronic Venous Disease (CVD)
chronic_venous_disease.description: This flowchart outlines the diagnostic and treatment pathways for patients with suspected chronic venous disease (CVD) of the lower limbs. It includes steps for clinical examination, diagnostic imaging, and potential interventions based on the findings.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Figure 6
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Conservative Management of Chronic Venous Disease Flowchart
chronic_venous_disease.description: This flowchart outlines the conservative management strategies for patients with symptomatic chronic venous disease (C0s – C5) who are not undergoing interventional treatment, are awaiting intervention, or have persisting symptoms post-intervention. It includes recommendations for exercise, lifestyle adaptations, compression treatment, and pharmacotherapy.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Figure 19
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Interventional Treatment Options for SSV Incompetence
chronic_venous_disease.description: This flowchart outlines the interventional treatment options for patients with symptomatic small saphenous vein (SSV) incompetence. It emphasizes shared decision-making and provides various treatment paths based on the involvement of the SSV trunk and tributaries.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Figure 22
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Interventional Options for AASV Incompetence
chronic_venous_disease.description: This flowchart outlines treatment options for patients with symptomatic anterior accessory saphenous vein (AASV) incompetence. It emphasizes shared decision-making for both AASV trunk and tributaries, with specific interventions like EVTA and UGFS highlighted.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Figure 28
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Management Strategy for Symptomatic Deep Venous Obstruction and Reflux
chronic_venous_disease.description: This flowchart outlines the management strategy for patients with symptomatic chronic venous disease and deep venous pathology. It emphasizes patient selection, type of pathology, and shared decision-making to determine treatment paths, including interventional treatment and surgical repair.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Figure 29
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Management Strategy for Deep Venous Obstruction
chronic_venous_disease.description: This flowchart outlines the interventional treatment strategy for patients with deep venous obstruction and severe symptoms. It emphasizes a multidisciplinary approach, prioritizes endovascular treatment, and highlights the need for post-procedure surveillance.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Figure 31
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Management Strategy for Active Venous Leg Ulceration (C6)
chronic_venous_disease.description: This flowchart outlines the management strategy for patients with active venous leg ulceration classified as CEAP clinical class C6. It emphasizes prompt assessment, multidisciplinary management, and coordination between healthcare providers. Key interventions include arterial assessment, treating venous hypertension, compression therapy, and additional interventions like pharmacotherapy and lifestyle modifications.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Figure 32
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Management of Superficial Reflux and Compression in Venous Leg Ulceration
chronic_venous_disease.description: This flowchart outlines the treatment strategies for patients with healed (C5) or active (C6) venous leg ulceration. It details the management of superficial reflux and compression therapy, providing recommendations based on clinical class and evidence levels.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Figure 37
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Management of Symptomatic Varicose Veins with Pelvic Venous Disorders Flowchart
chronic_venous_disease.description: This flowchart outlines the management of patients with symptomatic varicose veins and suspected pelvic venous disorders. It includes diagnostic steps using duplex ultrasound and transvaginal ultrasound, and treatment options based on patient status and symptoms.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Figure 43
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Clinical Presentations of Chronic Venous Disease in Limbs
chronic_venous_disease.description: This figure illustrates various clinical manifestations of chronic venous disease in the lower limbs. It includes images of telangiectasias, varicose veins, swelling, pigmentation, and skin changes due to venous insufficiency.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Figure 44
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Conservative Management of Chronic Venous Disease
chronic_venous_disease.description: This flowchart outlines the conservative management strategies for patients with symptomatic chronic venous disease (C0s–C5) who are not undergoing interventional treatment. It includes exercise, lifestyle adaptations, compression treatment, and pharmacotherapy with venous active drugs (VADs).

chronic_venous_disease.label: Figure 45
chronic_venous_disease.caption: Superficial Vein Treatment and Compression Strategies for Venous Ulceration (C5/C6)
chronic_venous_disease.description: This flowchart outlines the treatment strategies for patients with healed (C5) or active (C6) venous leg ulceration. It details the management of superficial reflux and compression, providing recommendations based on clinical class and evidence level.

chronic_venous_disease.label: Figure 33
chronic_venous_disease.caption: The four anatomical zones 1-4 of the female abdomen and pelvis, where the “SVP” classification for pelvic venous disorders can be applied. Symptomes (S), Varices (V), and Pathophysiology (P)
chronic_venous_disease.description: Anatomical diagram defining the four clinical zones (1–4) of the female abdomen and pelvis used in the SVP classification system. This framework organizes Pelvic Venous Disorders (PeVD) by correlating clinical Symptoms (S), the presence of Varices (V), and underlying Pathophysiology (P) with specific anatomical regions to guide diagnosis and treatment.

venous_thrombosis.label: Table 7
venous_thrombosis.caption: Wells Score for DVT Prediction
venous_thrombosis.description: This table outlines the Wells score criteria for predicting the likelihood of lower extremity deep vein thrombosis (DVT). Each clinical characteristic is assigned a score, which is summed to determine the probability of DVT. A higher total score indicates a higher probability of DVT.

venous_thrombosis.label: Table 20
venous_thrombosis.caption: APTT Dose Adjustment Table
venous_thrombosis.description: This table provides guidelines for adjusting anticoagulant doses based on activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) values. It outlines specific dose adjustments for different APTT ranges to maintain therapeutic levels.

venous_thrombosis.label: Table 29
venous_thrombosis.caption: Cumulative Incidence of Recurrent VTE After Anticoagulation Cessation
venous_thrombosis.description: This table presents the cumulative incidence of recurrent venous thromboembolism (VTE) in men and women after stopping anticoagulation therapy. It provides percentages along with 95% confidence intervals for different follow-up periods.

venous_thrombosis.label: Table 33
venous_thrombosis.caption: Timing of Last DOAC Intake Before Elective Intervention
venous_thrombosis.description: This table provides guidance on the timing of the last intake of direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) before an elective intervention, based on creatinine clearance levels and bleeding risk. It specifies different time frames for Dabigatran and Apixaban/Edoxaban/Rivaroxaban, considering both low and high bleeding risks.

venous_thrombosis.label: Table 43
venous_thrombosis.caption: Post-Thrombotic Syndrome Severity Scoring Table
venous_thrombosis.description: This table outlines the scoring system for assessing the severity of post-thrombotic syndrome (PTS) based on subjective symptoms and objective clinical signs. Each symptom or sign is scored from 0 (absent) to 3 (severe), with the total score determining the PTS severity level.

venous_thrombosis.label: Table 50
venous_thrombosis.caption: Randomised Controlled Trials for Calf Deep Vein Thrombosis
venous_thrombosis.description: This table summarizes randomized controlled trials involving patients with calf deep vein thrombosis (DVT). It compares different interventions, control groups, and outcomes to assess the effectiveness of various anticoagulation therapies.

venous_thrombosis.label: Table 54
venous_thrombosis.caption: Risk Factors for Recurrent Venous Thromboembolism in Calf DVT Patients
venous_thrombosis.description: This table outlines the risk factors associated with recurrent venous thromboembolism in patients with calf deep vein thrombosis. It compares annual recurrence rates and adjusted hazard ratios for different clinical parameters, highlighting significant differences based on age, sex, and type of thrombosis.

venous_thrombosis.label: Table 69
venous_thrombosis.caption: Forest Plot Analysis of DOACs vs. Dalteparin for Cancer-Associated VTE
venous_thrombosis.description: This table presents a forest plot analysis comparing direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) - edoxaban, rivaroxaban, and apixaban - with dalteparin for treating cancer-associated venous thrombosis. It shows the risk ratios and confidence intervals for each drug, indicating their effectiveness relative to dalteparin.

venous_thrombosis.label: Figure 4
venous_thrombosis.caption: Algorithm for Elastic Compression Stockings Treatment
venous_thrombosis.description: This flowchart outlines the decision-making process for using elastic compression stockings (ECS) based on Villalta scores at different time intervals after deep vein thrombosis. The algorithm suggests stopping or continuing ECS based on the score, with follow-up ending after 24 months.

venous_thrombosis.label: Figure 8
venous_thrombosis.caption: Flowchart for Provoked DVT Treatment Recommendations
venous_thrombosis.description: This flowchart outlines the treatment recommendations for provoked deep vein thrombosis (DVT) of the lower extremity, excluding cases related to pregnancy or cancer. It includes risk stratification for bleeding and provides guidance on anticoagulation therapy, compression methods, and management of recurrent DVT.

venous_thrombosis.label: Figure 9
venous_thrombosis.caption: Flowchart for Unprovoked DVT Treatment Recommendations
venous_thrombosis.description: This flowchart outlines the treatment recommendations for unprovoked deep vein thrombosis (DVT) of the lower extremity. It includes risk stratification for bleeding and provides guidance on anticoagulation therapy, compression therapy, and further testing based on risk levels.

venous_thrombosis.label: Figure 12
venous_thrombosis.caption: Superficial Vein Thrombosis Treatment Flowchart
venous_thrombosis.description: This flowchart outlines the recommended treatment pathway for patients with suspected superficial vein thrombosis. It includes steps for diagnosis using ultrasound and subsequent treatment options based on the location and extent of the thrombosis.

venous_thrombosis.label: Figure 14
venous_thrombosis.caption: Forest Plot Analysis of DOACs vs. Dalteparin for Cancer-Associated Thrombosis
venous_thrombosis.description: This figure presents a forest plot comparing the risk of major bleeding in cancer patients treated with direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) versus dalteparin. The analysis includes three DOACs: edoxaban, rivaroxaban, and apixaban, with data from multiple trials. Risk ratios and confidence intervals are provided for each comparison.

vascular_trauma.label: Table 6
vascular_trauma.caption: ESVS Levels of Evidence Grading System
vascular_trauma.description: This table outlines the European Society for Vascular Surgery's grading system for levels of evidence in clinical practice guidelines. It categorizes evidence into three levels (A, B, C) based on the quality and type of studies.

vascular_trauma.label: Table 27
vascular_trauma.caption: Management of Carotid and Vertebral Artery Injuries by ESVS Grade
vascular_trauma.description: This table outlines the recommended management strategies for carotid and vertebral artery injuries based on the ESVS grading system. It provides specific treatment options for different grades of arterial injury, ranging from partial wall injuries to complete occlusions.

vascular_trauma.label: Table 48
vascular_trauma.caption: Management of Blunt Thoracic Aortic Injury by Grade
vascular_trauma.description: This table outlines the suggested management strategies for blunt thoracic aortic injury (BTAI) based on the grade of aortic injury. It categorizes the injuries into three grades, detailing the description and recommended management for each.

vascular_trauma.label: Table 71
vascular_trauma.caption: Abdominal Arterial Injury Management Table
vascular_trauma.description: This table outlines the management strategies for different grades of abdominal arterial injuries, categorized by the ESVS grading system. It provides specific recommendations for the common and external iliac arteries, superior mesenteric artery, and renal artery based on the severity of the injury.

vascular_trauma.label: Figure 1
vascular_trauma.caption: Vascular Injury Grading Diagram
vascular_trauma.description: This diagram illustrates different grades of vascular injury, showing the progression from minor to severe damage. Grade 1 depicts a minor tear, Grade 2 shows a more pronounced injury with vessel dilation, and Grade 3 illustrates a severe rupture with significant bleeding.

vascular_trauma.label: Figure 3
vascular_trauma.caption: ESVS Classification of Traumatic Thoracic Aortic Injury
vascular_trauma.description: This diagram illustrates the European Society for Vascular Surgery (ESVS) classification of traumatic thoracic aortic injury. It depicts three grades of injury, ranging from intimal or vessel wall injury with normal external contour to more severe disruptions.

vascular_trauma.label: Figure 4
vascular_trauma.caption: Follow-Up Imaging Protocol for BTAI
vascular_trauma.description: This flowchart outlines the recommended imaging follow-up for patients with blunt thoracic aortic injury (BTAI) based on the type of repair: open surgical, endovascular, or no repair. It specifies the timing and type of imaging (CTA or MRA) required at different intervals.

vascular_trauma.label: Figure 5
vascular_trauma.caption: Medial Visceral Rotations for Retroperitoneal Access
vascular_trauma.description: This diagram illustrates the right and left medial visceral rotations used for retroperitoneal exploration. It shows the anatomical shifts necessary to access the retroperitoneal space during surgical procedures.

vascular_trauma.label: Figure 8
vascular_trauma.caption: Illustration of Blood Vessel Injury with Bleeding
vascular_trauma.description: This diagram shows a cross-section of a blood vessel with a visible injury leading to bleeding. It highlights the disruption in the vessel wall and the escape of blood, illustrating the potential for clot formation and blockage.

vascular_trauma.label: Figure 9
vascular_trauma.caption: Applying Pressure to Control Bleeding
vascular_trauma.description: The figure illustrates a person applying direct pressure to a bleeding wound on another individual's leg. This is a basic first aid technique used to control bleeding in cases of vascular trauma.

vascular_trauma.label: Figure 2
vascular_trauma.caption: Grade 1 injury confined to intima as per ESVS grading system for arterial trauma
vascular_trauma.description: Clinical illustration or imaging demonstrating a Grade 1 arterial injury based on the ESVS classification for arterial trauma. This grade is defined by a focal injury strictly confined to the intima, such as a small intimal tear or flap, without disruption of the media or adventitia and with preserved distal flow.
